label: Glucose
summary: Primary carbon energy source transported into cells via GLUT1–4 facilitated diffusion transporters. Glucose fuels the Warburg effect in cancer cells, where aerobic glycolysis is preferred even with functional mitochondria. Hexokinase commits glucose to glycolysis by phosphorylating it to G-6-P.
citation: Vander Heiden MG et al. (2009). Understanding the Warburg effect: the metabolic requirements of cell proliferation. Science 324:1029-1033.

label: Glycogen
summary: Branched glucose polymer serving as the primary intracellular carbohydrate reserve. Glycogen phosphorylase cleaves glucose-1-phosphate, which isomerizes to G-6-P connecting glycogenolysis to glycolysis (step d). Its synthesis and breakdown are reciprocally regulated by insulin and glucagon.
citation: Roach PJ et al. (2012). Glycogen and its metabolism: some new developments and old themes. Chem Rev 112:4541-4546.

label: G-6-P
summary: Glucose-6-phosphate is the first committed glycolytic intermediate, produced by hexokinase at the cost of one ATP. It is a critical branchpoint metabolite that can enter glycolysis, the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) for NADPH and ribose-5-phosphate, or glycogen synthesis. Accumulation allosterically inhibits hexokinase.
citation: Berg JM et al. (2015). Biochemistry 8th ed. Chapter 16: Glycolysis and Gluconeogenesis.

label: DHAP
summary: Dihydroxyacetone phosphate is one of two triose phosphates released by aldolase cleavage of fructose-1,6-bisphosphate. Triose phosphate isomerase (TPI) equilibrates DHAP with G3P. The glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle uses DHAP as the cytoplasmic electron acceptor, coupling cytoplasmic NADH oxidation to the mitochondrial respiratory chain.
citation: Mráček T et al. (2013). The function of the mitochondrial glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase in mammalian tissues. Biochim Biophys Acta 1827:401-410.

label: G3P
summary: D-Glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate is the glycolytically active triose phosphate oxidised by GAPDH to 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate. This step couples substrate-level phosphorylation to NAD⁺ regeneration, the key thermodynamic driver of glycolytic flux. GAPDH moonlights as a nuclear protein regulating transcription under oxidative stress.
citation: Seidler NW (2013). GAPDH: Biological Properties and Diversity. Adv Exp Med Biol 985.

label: Glutamine
summary: The most abundant plasma amino acid and the principal anaplerotic substrate for rapidly proliferating cells. Glutamine is imported via SLC1A5 (ASCT2) and SLC38A1 and fuels the TCA cycle via glutaminolysis (step c). It is the nitrogen donor for nucleotide and hexosamine biosynthesis and supports redox balance through glutathione synthesis.
citation: Altman BJ et al. (2016). From Krebs to clinic: glutamine metabolism to cancer therapy. Nat Rev Cancer 16:619-634.

label: Glutamate
summary: Glutamate is the universal amino-group donor in transamination reactions and a key excitatory neurotransmitter. It is produced from glutamine by GLS1/GLS2 glutaminases. GLUD1 or aminotransferases then convert it to α-KG, directly linking the amino acid pool to the TCA cycle and supporting tumour growth.
citation: Matés JM et al. (2020). Glutaminase isoenzymes as key regulators in metabolic and oxidative stress against cancer. Mol Aspects Med 70:48-63.

label: α-KG
summary: Alpha-ketoglutarate (2-oxoglutarate) is a pivotal TCA cycle intermediate and obligate co-substrate for the α-KG-dependent dioxygenase superfamily — including TET DNA demethylases, JmjC histone demethylases, and EglN prolyl hydroxylases — directly coupling metabolic state to epigenetic reprogramming. IDH1/2 mutations convert it to the oncometabolite 2-HG.
citation: Xu W et al. (2011). Oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate is a competitive inhibitor of α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases. Cancer Cell 19:17-30.

label: Glycerol
summary: Glycerol is the lipid backbone released by lipolysis of triglycerides in adipose tissue. Imported via AQP3, AQP7, and AQP9 aquaporins plus SLC transporters (step b), it is phosphorylated by glycerol kinase to Gro3P, entering both lipid synthesis and the glycolytic triose phosphate pool. A key gluconeogenic substrate in the fasted state.
citation: Patsouris D et al. (2016). Aquaporins in the adipose tissue of normal and obese mice. J Physiol Biochem 72:529-537.

label: Gro3P
summary: sn-Glycerol-3-phosphate bridges lipid anabolism and carbohydrate catabolism. Cytoplasmic GPD1 reduces DHAP to Gro3P using NADH. Mitochondrial GPD2 reoxidises it via ubiquinone, completing the glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle that transfers cytoplasmic reducing equivalents into the mitochondrial electron transport chain.
citation: Mráček T et al. (2013). The function and role of the mitochondrial glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase. Biochim Biophys Acta 1827:401-410.

label: Pyruvate
summary: Pyruvate is the terminal glycolytic product and the central metabolic decision node. Under normoxia it is imported into mitochondria and decarboxylated to acetyl-CoA by the PDC. Under hypoxia or Warburg conditions, LDHA reduces it to lactate to regenerate NAD⁺ for sustained glycolysis. HIF-1α-induced PDK1 phosphorylates and inactivates PDC, enforcing the Warburg switch.
citation: Vander Heiden MG et al. (2009). Understanding the Warburg effect. Science 324:1029-1033.

label: Acetyl-CoA
summary: Acetyl-CoA is the central mitochondrial substrate linking catabolism to the TCA cycle. The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) irreversibly generates it from pyruvate; PDK1/PDK2, upregulated by HIF-1α under hypoxia, phosphorylates and inactivates PDC. Nuclear ACLY-derived acetyl-CoA is the obligate substrate for histone acetyltransferases, coupling nutrient status to chromatin acetylation.
citation: Kim JW et al. (2006). HIF-1-mediated expression of pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase: a metabolic switch for cellular adaptation to hypoxia. Cell Metab 3:177-185.

label: Malate
summary: L-Malate is a TCA cycle dicarboxylate oxidised by MDH2 to oxaloacetate in the mitochondrial matrix. The malate-aspartate shuttle uses malate export to transfer mitochondrial reducing equivalents across the inner membrane. Malic enzymes ME1/ME2 also convert malate to pyruvate, regenerating NADPH for lipid synthesis and redox defence.
citation: Zhao Y & Bhatt DL (2017). Metabolic pathways linking mitochondrial function to epigenetics. Nat Cell Biol 19:1048-1057.

label: TCA Cycle
summary: The TCA cycle (Krebs cycle) operates in the mitochondrial matrix and oxidises acetyl-CoA through eight steps, generating 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, and 1 GTP per turn. It provides biosynthetic precursors — citrate for lipogenesis, α-KG for amino-acid and epigenetic regulation, succinyl-CoA for haem synthesis, and OAA for gluconeogenesis. Oncogenic IDH1/2, SDH, and FH mutations dysregulate TCA metabolites.
citation: Owen OE et al. (2002). The key role of anaplerosis and cataplerosis for citric acid cycle function. J Biol Chem 277:30409-30412.

label: Lactate
summary: Lactate is generated from pyruvate by LDHA (step 1 forward) and reconverted by LDHB in oxidative tissues. Beyond a glycolytic end product, the lactate shuttle posits that MCT4-expressing glycolytic cells export lactate for MCT1-expressing oxidative cells to consume. Lactate activates GPR81 signalling and is the direct precursor for lactyl-CoA-mediated epigenetic modifications.
citation: Brooks GA (2018). The science and translation of lactate shuttle theory. Cell Metab 27:757-785.

label: Gluconeogenesis
summary: Hepatic gluconeogenesis recycles lactate to glucose via the Cori cycle (step 2), transferring the metabolic cost of anaerobic glycolysis to the liver. Key regulated enzymes PEPCK1, FBPase-1, and G6Pase are transcriptionally activated by FOXO1 and CREB. During exercise hyperlactatemia, elevated gluconeogenic flux sustains blood glucose and replenishes glycogen stores.
citation: Cori GT & Cori CF (1929). Glycogen formation in the liver from d- and l-lactic acid. J Biol Chem 81:389-403.

label: Lactyl-CoA
summary: S-Lactoyl-CoA is the activated thioester of lactate generated by ACSS1 (mitochondrial) and ACSS2 (cytoplasmic/nuclear, step 3). It is the lactyl-group donor for both histone and non-histone protein lactylation catalysed by p300 and PCAF acetyltransferases, directly bridging Warburg metabolism to epigenetic gene regulation.
citation: Zhang D et al. (2019). Metabolic regulation of gene expression by histone lactylation. Nature 574:575-580.

label: Histone Lactylation
summary: Histone lactylation (H3K18la, H4K12la) is a PTM where p300/PCAF acetyltransferases transfer the lactyl group from lactyl-CoA to lysine ε-amines on histones. HDAC1/3 are the eraser enzymes. Elevated in M2-polarised macrophages and multiple cancers, it activates homeostatic gene programmes and epigenetically links the Warburg effect to transcriptional reprogramming.
citation: Zhang D et al. (2019). Metabolic regulation of gene expression by histone lactylation. Nature 574:575-580.

label: Non-histone Lactylation
summary: Over 2,000 lysine lactylation sites have been identified by mass spectrometry on non-histone proteins including metabolic enzymes (ALDOA, ENO1, GAPDH), nuclear proteins (HMGB1, PCNA), and cytoskeletal components. Non-histone lactylation modulates enzymatic activity, protein–protein interactions, and subcellular localisation, extending the Warburg-to-proteome regulatory axis.
citation: Yu J et al. (2021). Histone lactylation drives oncogenesis by facilitating m6A reader YTHDF2 in ocular melanoma. Genome Biol 22:85.

label: Hypoxia
summary: Hypoxia (O₂ < 1–2%) stabilises HIF-1α by preventing VHL-mediated ubiquitination. HIF-1α transactivates PDK1, which phosphorylates and inactivates the PDC E1α subunit, blocking pyruvate entry into the mitochondria. HIF-1α simultaneously upregulates LDHA and MCT4, maximising lactate production and export. This switch is constitutively active in cancer cells with oncogenic RAS or MYC.
citation: Papandreou I et al. (2006). HIF-1 mediates adaptation to hypoxia by downregulating mitochondrial oxygen consumption. Cell Metab 3:187-197.
